questions.question: Why is a browser agent a uniquely dangerous category of long-horizon agent?
options: Browsers are slow to automate
It reads untrusted content and takes consequential actions — every page is input the user didn't write, and every form is a potential command channel
Browsers require constant internet
It can only run one tab at a time
questions.explanation: The 2025-2026 attack corpus (Tainted Memories, HashJack, Comet one-click hijacks) is real, not hypothetical. The attack lives in the agent's reading-vs-acting boundary, which is architecturally fuzzy.

questions.question: Why did OpenAI's head of preparedness say indirect prompt injection "is not a bug that can be fully patched"?
options: The codebase is too large to fix
The attack is isomorphic to the agent's capability — it must read untrusted content to work, and any content it reads could contain instructions it follows
Browsers refuse security updates
It only affects open-source agents
questions.explanation: Same reasoning shape as Lob's theorem: the agent can't prove the next token is safe. Defenses (trust boundaries, allowlists, HITL) raise attack cost and shrink blast radius, but they don't close the class.

questions.question: What do BrowseComp, OSWorld, and WebArena-Verified each measure, and why does it matter which you cite?
options: They are three names for the same benchmark
Fact-finding on the open web, operating a full desktop, and finishing transactional web flows respectively — a high BrowseComp score says nothing about whether the agent can book a flight
They all measure raw token throughput
Only OSWorld measures anything real
questions.explanation: Different axes, different horizons. WebArena-Verified (ICLR 2026) also fixed an ~11.3% false-negative rate in the original WebArena and added a 258-task Hard subset. Production decisions need the benchmark matching the task distribution.

questions.question: What is a memory-binding attack on a browser agent?
options: Overflowing the agent's RAM
A page instructs the agent to write a persistent memory; next session the memory fires the payload with no visible trigger
Encrypting the agent's memory for ransom
Filling the context window with junk
questions.explanation: The Tainted Memories class weaponizes durable state (Lesson 12): the attack persists across sessions and detonates later. The defense includes canary tokens on memory so a firing entry is visible to the user.

questions.question: What is the load-bearing principle of a shipping browser-agent defense posture?
options: Block all JavaScript
The read/write boundary: reading is never consequential, but writing (form submit, side-effecting tool) triggered by out-of-trust-boundary content requires fresh human approval
Run the agent as root for full visibility
Trust pages from HTTPS sites
questions.explanation: Reading untrusted content is unavoidable; acting on it is the gate. Combined with per-task tool allowlists, scoped-credential session isolation, content sanitizers, and HITL on consequential actions, it reduces blast radius without pretending to close the injection class.
